coredata.dc:title: Curcuma amada derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a dual sensor for tetracycline and mercury ions
coredata.dc:creator: Korah, Binila K.
Punnoose, Mamatha Susan
Thara, Chinnu R.
Abraham, Thomas
Ambady, K.G.
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: The hydrothermal approach was used to make nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) employing ginger mango and triethylene tetraamine as precursors. The as-synthesized NCDs with a size of 7 nm exhibited good aqueous dispersibility, photostability and green fluorescence under UV light. Various optical, morphological and electrochemical properties of NCDs were examined. It was used as a dual-mode probe for tetracycline and mercuric ion detection. It detected tetracycline selectively and sensitively using an inner filter-based fluorescence quenching mechanism with a detection limit of 32 nM. The NCD modified glassy carbon electrode detected mercuric ions electrochemically with a detection limit of 0.2 nM. Moreover, NCDs were utilized to sense tetracyclines and mercuric ions in tap and river water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Curcuma amada
Antibiotics
Fluorescence
Electrochemical analysis
Tetracyclines
Mercury ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of NCDs
Detection of tetracycline by NCDs through fluorescence method
Fluorescence sensing mechanism for tetracycline determination
Electrochemical behavior of NCD/GCE towards the detection of Hg2+ ions
Detection of Hg2+ions by NCDs through electrochemical method
Mechanism of electrochemical detection of Hg2+ ions via NCD/GCE sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of NCDs. (b) HR-TEM image of NCDs. (c) SAED pattern of NCDs, and (d) particle size distribution histogram of NCDs.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of NCDs. (b) XRD spectrum of NCDs. (c) Raman spectrum of NCDs, and (d) TGA and DTG of NCDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of NCDs and High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s (c) N1s (d) Ols of NCDs.
(a) UV–vis spectrum of NCDs. (b) Emission spectrum of NCDs, when excited at 370 nm, and (c) when excited at different wavelengths.
Fluorescence spectra showing (a) photostability, (b) ionic stability, (c) pH stability and (d) storage stability of NCDs.

(a) Fluorescence response of NCDs in the presence of tetracycline. (b) Relationship between fluorescence intensity and different concentrations of tetracycline, and (c) selectivity study of tetracycline in the presence of (1) levofloxacin, (2) amoxicillin, (3) cloxacillin, (4) vancomycin, (5) epinephrine, (6) fluorouracil and (7) ciprofloxacin.
(a) Absorption spectrum of tetracycline and excitation spectrum of NCDs, and (b) fluorescence lifetime of NCDs in the absence and presence of tetracycline.
(a) CV runs of bare GCE, NCD/GCE and NCD/GCE/Hg(II) in PBS, (b) effect of pH, (c) EIS spectra of NCD/GCE and (d) amperometric current-time response curves for the successive addition of Hg(II) ions.

(a) DPV of NCD/GCE with Hg(II) concentrations from 1 to 10 nM in PBS solution (b) plot of concentration of Hg(II) vs. peak current (c) effect of scan rate, (d) selectivity study, and (e) CV runs showing the stability of the sensor.
Preparation of NCDs for functioning as a fluorescent probe for tetracycline detection and electrochemical probe for Hg(II) detection.
Schematic diagram illustrating the preparation process of NCDs from Curcuma amada and triethylene tetraamine.
Electron transfer mechanism for the sensing of Hg2+ ions on the surface of NCD/GCE.
Comparison of different fluorescent methods for tetracycline sensing.
Comparison of various methods for the mercuric ion detection.
The detection of tetracyclines in real samples through fluorescence analysis.
The detection of Hg2+ ions in real samples through electrochemical analysis.

head.ce:title: Curcuma amada derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a dual sensor for tetracycline and mercury ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Priyadarsini Hills P. O, Kottayam 686 560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Priyadarsini Hills P. O, Kottayam-686 560, Kerala, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Catholicate College, Pathanamthitta 689 645, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Catholicate College, Pathanamthitta-689 645, Kerala, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Special Education, National Institute for the Empowerment of Persons with Intellectual Disabilities, Telangana, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institute for the Empowerment of Persons with Intellectual Disabilities

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Special Education, National Institute for the Empowerment of Persons with Intellectual Disabilities, Telangana, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The hydrothermal approach was used to make nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) employing ginger mango and triethylene tetraamine as precursors. The as-synthesized NCDs with a size of 7 nm exhibited good aqueous dispersibility, photostability and green fluorescence under UV light. Various optical, morphological and electrochemical properties of NCDs were examined. It was used as a dual-mode probe for tetracycline and mercuric ion detection. It detected tetracycline selectively and sensitively using an inner filter-based fluorescence quenching mechanism with a detection limit of 32 nM. The NCD modified glassy carbon electrode detected mercuric ions electrochemically with a detection limit of 0.2 nM. Moreover, NCDs were utilized to sense tetracyclines and mercuric ions in tap and river water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are new nanomaterials in the nanocarbon family with remarkable qualities like high water solubility, optical stability, excellent biocompatibility, and ease of manufacture. Since their accidental discovery during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) in 2004, CDs with size ranges below 10 nm have risen in popularity owing to their extraordinary properties [1–3]. Because of their exceptional advantages, they have piqued the interest of both the academic and commercial communities [4–11]. In all fields, including photocatalysis, carbon-based nanomaterials have received more attention [39–41]. Their investigation, synthesis, and innovation take place in a variety of areas, and studies involving the development of carbon-based materials have generated a number of valuable discoveries for broad range of contexts, allowing the production of a number of materials with various applications. Compared to metal-semiconductor quantum dots and amorphous silicon quantum dots, CDs have various advantages, including minimal toxicity and low cost. Nowadays, CDs are employed to detect hazardous materials, including drugs, heavy metal ions, pesticides, etc.

Tetracyclines are antibiotics that are commonly used in pharmaceuticals and food preservatives. Despite their broad antibacterial range, their residues in food and the environment stance a significant threat to all living things [12–14]. Due to their extreme toxicity, mercuric ions are among the most dangerous heavy metal ions. Recent research has found that long-term exposure to relatively low levels of mercury is damaging to humans' immune and nervous systems, which has been related to different health problems, including Alzheimer's disease, nephrotic syndrome, and respiratory diseases [15,16]. The drawbacks of existing technology prompted scientists to consider novel sensing methods that are cost-effective, safe, and user-friendly [17–19]. Because of their remarkable selectivity and sensitivity, fluorescence spectroscopy and electrochemical sensing methods have recently gained popularity.

This study created a novel carbon dot-based sensor for fluorescence and electrochemical sensing of tetracycline and mercuric ions. Hydrothermal synthesis was used to make nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs), utilizing ginger mango and triethylene tetraamine as carbon and nitrogen precursors. Curcuma amada, sometimes known as ginger mango, is a Zingiberaceae plant closely related to turmeric (Curcuma longa). The rhizomes have a raw mango aroma and are quite comparable to traditional ginger. However, they miss the pungency of common ginger. Starch, phenolic acids, volatile oils, curcuminoids, and terpenoids are the most important chemical components [20]. The green fluorescent NCDs functioned well as a dual-sensing probe for tetracycline and mercuric ions, with a good detection limit. NCDs were used as a fluorescent probe to detect tetracycline via a fluorescence quenching mechanism, and an NCD-modified glassy carbon electrode electrochemically detected Hg2+ ions with high selectivity (Scheme 1 ). In the following aspects, our work is unique and has various advantages over other works [42,43]: (i) NCDs are synthesized from a readily available green source, (ii) no complicated procedures are used in the fabrication of this sensor, and (iii) the sensor contains no metals and hence may be used on real samples with minimal toxicity.

Curcuma amada (ginger mango) was collected from a local market. Triethylene tetraamine (TETA), potassium chloride, sodium phosphate dibasic, dichloromethane, quinine sulfate, sodium phosphate monobasic, hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide were obtained from Merck, India. The antibiotics used were tetracycline, levofloxacin, amoxicillin, cloxacillin, vancomycin, epinephrine, flurouracil, and ciprofloxacin. CdCl2.H2O, FeSO4.7H2O, HgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, K2Cr2O7, CoCl2.6H2O, ZnSO4.7H2O, NiCl2.6H2O, CuSO4.5H2O, ascorbic acid, cysteine, arginine, leucine, phenylalanine, valine, and glucose were also utilized in interference investigations. Double distilled water was employed for the whole experiment.

A hydrothermal approach was used to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon dots. Curcuma amada extract and triethylene tetraamine were thoroughly mixed for 15 min in a conical flask. It was placed in a teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 7 h. It was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and stored at 4 °C after being washed with dichloromethane.

FTIR spectrum was acquired on a (Perkin Elmer-400) spectrometer with ATR attachment. UV–vis spectral analysis was performed using the spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu Japan). The photoluminescence spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorometer. A PANalytic X'PERT-PRO X-ray spectrometer was used to measure the XRD. A JEOL JEM-2100 microscope was employed to obtain high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HR-TEM) pictures. The particle size and zeta potential measurements were performed using a Horiba SZ-100 scientific nanoparticle analyzer (Japan). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the samples was done using a Perkin Elmer TGA-8000 apparatus under synthetic airflow at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 over a temperature range of 25 to 700 °C. Thermo Scientific ESCALAB spectrometer was used to obtain X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies. All electrochemical tests were conducted with the help of a three-electrode setup in the Biologic SP 200 workstation. Glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was employed as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, and Pt wire as the counter electrode.

Different tetracycline concentrations (0 to 20 μM, 100 μL each) were added after mixing 300 μL of N-CDs solution with 3 mL of distilled water. The solutions were ultrasonicated for 10 min at room temperature and then let to stand for 5 min before the fluorescence spectra were recorded. With a 370 nm excitation, the emission spectra were recorded at room temperature. In the absence of antibiotics, the fluorescence intensity is denoted as F0. In contrast, in the presence of antibiotics, it is designated as F. Other antibiotics, amino acids, and interfering compounds were used to test the probe's specificity.

Before the experiment started, the bare GCE was polished with alumina slurry (0.05 μM). Drops of crude carbon dot solution were dropped onto the electrode's surface and left to dry at ambient temperature. With 0.1 M buffer solution at pH 7, varied concentrations of Hg2+ were prepared. Differential pulse voltammetry was performed at varied concentrations of Hg2+ using the three-electrode technique (10 to 60 nM). The sensor's selectivity was also analyzed using cyclic voltammetry in the presence of other metal ions.

The potential real-world application performance of NCDs in tap water and river water samples was evaluated. Three separate samples were made by adding analytes (tetracycline and Hg2+) in varying amounts to the real water samples, and then fluorescence and electrochemical measurements were done on these samples. The real water samples were collected and centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 15 min before filtration through a 0.22 μm membrane. The resulting water was collected and spiked with three different analyte concentrations.

NCDs were prepared through hydrothermal method from Curcuma amada and triethylene tetraamine (TETA). The preparation approach is presented in Scheme 2 . Then it was characterized using a set of techniques. The generation of CDs was verified using UV–vis spectral measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: NCDs were prepared from ginger mango and triethylene tetraamine by a simple hydrothermal method. Several characterization techniques were employed for determining the morphological and optical features of NCDs. Representative images of TEM, SAED pattern, and histogram of spherical NCDs with monodisperse distribution are shown in Fig. 1 . Graphitic carbon's (100) plane was confirmed from the interlayer fringe width of 0.22 nm in the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1b). The average diameter of NCDs was 7 nm, which was nearly the same as that of the result obtained from the dynamic light scattering experiment (Fig. S1).

FTIR studies of NCDs confirmed the presence of alcohols, amines, carbonyl groups, and aromatic rings on the surface of NCDs (Fig. 2a). Strong peaks at 3400 cm−1 and 3284 cm−1 correspond to the -OH and NH stretching vibrations. The vibrational bands at 2932 cm−1 and in the range of 1040 to 1500 cm−1 demonstrate the presence of aliphatic CH and C-N/C-O functionalities [26,27]. The hydrophilic nature and stability in aqueous solution directly indicate the presence of surface functional groups on NCDs. A broad peak at 2ϴ =22 0 in the XRD spectrum shows the amorphous nature of NCDs [22] (Fig. 2b).

Further characterizations of elements in NCDs were performed using XPS measurements. Three strong peaks attributing to O1s, N1s and C1s were observed at 530.6, 397.5, and 284.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 3 ). When the C1s spectrum was deconvoluted, three peaks were observed at 284.6, 285.8 and 287.2 eV, indicating CC, CN and CO groups, respectively. N1s spectrum contained three peaks at 397.3, 398.5, 401.1 eV which was aasigned to C-N-C, NC3 and NH groups, respectively. Two peaks were observed for O1s spectrum which were at 530.4 and 531.9 eV, corresponding to CO and C-OH groups [21]. The abundance of carbon and oxygen elements in the NCDs was further confirmed by EDX analysis (Fig. S4).

Raman analysis was performed to know the extent of structural defects in NCDs. Two bands, namely D and G bands at 1331 cm−1 and 1559 cm−1 were observed in the Raman spectrum (Fig. 2c). The vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds on the termination plane of disordered graphite structure give rise to the D band. The G band is derived from graphite's E2g vibration mode, which is correlated with sp2 carbon atoms [23,24]. The synthesized NCDs have fewer defective carbon sites. The computed ID/IG intensity ratio was 0.85, revealing that the NCDs synthesized are of excellent quality. ID and IG denote the D and G bands' intensity ratio, respectively.

TGA analysis was used to evaluate the thermal stability of NCDs. Two major thermal breakdown events occurred in the ranges of 250–350 and 400–550 °C, as indicated in Fig. 2d. The loss of CO2 and evolution of other products with CO, CC, OH, CH, and CO linked to the surface of NCDs is attributed to thermal breakdown in the range 250–350 °C. The second thermal event is the degradation of carbonaceous materials present in NCDs [25]. This shows that NCDs are thermally stable at higher temperatures. The stability of NCDs in aqueous solutions was confirmed by the negative value of zeta potential as −16.1 mV (Fig. S2).

Absorption and fluorescence measurements were used to characterize NCDs optically. In the daylight, the NCD solution was pale yellow, but it was bright green under UV light. Fig. 4a and b show the absorption and fluorescence spectra of NCDs solution. NCDs showed a prominent peak at 350 nm and a small peak at 270 nm, indicating the presence of n-π* and π-π* transitions of the C=O/C=N and CC groups, respectively (Fig. 4a). On excitation, at 370 nm NCDs exhibited an emission at around 460 nm (Fig. 4b). Also, the emission was excitation-dependent (Fig. 4c).

NCDs must be analyzed for stability under various situations to determine their feasibility. After UV irradiation and storage for a long time, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs was measured. Under continuous UV irradiation for 60 min, NCDs sustained stable emission, demonstrating their outstanding photostability (Fig. 5a ). Also, after 2 weeks of storage, there was no noticeable change in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 5d). This proved that the NCDs produced have high photostability and storage stability. NCD's photoluminescence intensity was measured at various ionic strengths and pH levels to guarantee their stability. The carbon dot remained stable under different ionic strengths (Fig. 5b), and the intensity varied with pH, with the highest intensity obtained at acidic and neutral pH conditions (Fig. 5c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tetracycline by NCDs through fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: The reaction conditions were optimized for acquiring the best performance of the sensor. The impact of pH (3−12) on the probe's fluorescence intensity was investigated, and it was found that the process is pH sensitive. In the case of tetracycline antibiotics, fluorescence quenching efficiency increased with increasing pH, peaked at pH 7, and then declined. Henceforth pH 7 was chosen as the optimized pH for further experiments (Fig. S3). The emission wavelength of tetracycline was measured using fluorescence spectroscopy with 370 nm excitation. An increase in tetracycline concentration resulted in a decrease in fluorescence intensity. The selectivity of NCDs for these antibiotics was investigated in the presence of interfering substances, including antibiotics at the same concentration of 20 μΜ (Fig. 6c ). The findings reveal that tetracycline can be detected selectively even when other interfering compounds are present.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing mechanism for tetracycline determination
ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline sensing with NCDs was achieved through a fluorescence quenching mechanism. In the range of 0–16 μM, a linear relationship between quenching intensity and tetracycline concentrations was observed, with a lower limit of detection of 32 nM (Fig. 6a and b). A series of studies were conducted to study the quenching mechanism between NCDs and tetracycline. The absorption spectrum of tetracycline and the excitation spectrum of NCDs have a good spectral overlap, as shown in Fig. 7a. This supports the existence of two mechanisms: the inner filter effect (IFE) and/or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). FRET is a phenomenon of energy transfer between the excited state of a carbon dot and the ground state of the quencher. In contrast, IFE is an apparent quenching in which a second absorber filters out the emission, resulting in excited beam attenuation [28]. The best way to discern between IFE and FRET is to measure fluorescence lifetime. The fluorescence lifetime of the carbon dot-quencher system will decrease or rise in the event of FRET, whereas it will remain the same or nearly equal in the case of IFE [29,30]. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetimes of NCDs and the NCD-tetracycline system were measured, and the lifetime curves of both systems were found to be almost identical (Fig. 7b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of NCD/GCE towards the detection of Hg2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: Different electrochemical methods, including cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), amperometry, and electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) analysis, were used to study the electrochemical performance of NCDs. CV analysis of NCD/GCE, as shown in Fig. 8a, exhibited an increase in the current response than the bare GCE when it was scanned from the 0–0.8 V potential range at 70 mV/s scan rate. The maximum current response was obtained when the pH was 4 (Fig. 8b). Therefore pH 4 was chosen as the optimized pH for further electrochemical experiments. On the addition of Hg2+ ions (10 nM), two major peaks were observed at a pH of 4. The presence of NCDs and Hg2+ makes the bare GCE surface more conductive and surface functionalities on NCDs selectively bind with Hg2+ ions and promotes electron transfer, resulting in the appearance of redox peaks. The sensor's resistance is shown as a semicircle in the electrochemical impedance spectrum (Fig. 8c). Modified NCD/GCE showed decreased resistance when compared to the bare GCE. This demonstrates that the proposed sensor has a higher conductance and rate of transfer of electrons. The ability of the modified electrode to shine as an electrochemical probe for the sensing of Hg2+ is further verified by amperometric current-time response curves for the increasing addition of mercuric ions (Fig. 8d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ions by NCDs through electrochemical method
ce:section.ce:para: After the confirmation of NCD/GCE sensor ability as an electrochemical probe for Hg2+ ion detection, through CV, amperometry and EIS analysis, DPV analysis was also done. As demonstrated in Fig. 9a, the intensity of peak current rises with the concentration of Hg2+ ions (1–10 nM). A straight line graph was used to demonstrate the direct relationship between peak current with tetracycline concentration (Fig. 9b). 0.2 nM was determined to be the detection limit. The influence of scan rate on reaction kinetics was also tested, and it showed a linear response with the scan rate (20–140 mV/s) (Fig. 9c). This indicates that the process is reversible since there was no shift in the peak position. The selectivity of the new NCD/GCE sensor towards Hg2+ ions with other metal ions was investigated using cyclic voltammetric measurement. In the presence of Hg2+ ions, prominent and distinct peaks were observed compared to other metal ions, showing the sensor's selectivity (Fig. 9d). The fabricated NCD/GCE's reproducibility and stability were also explored (Fig. 9e). After one month of using the same electrode, a cyclic voltammogram of the freshly modified electrode in the voltage range of 0 to 0.8 V was recorded. There was no notable difference in peak current intensity. This shows the excellent stability of the sensor. Compared to earlier reports, the new sensor detects Hg2+ ions with great sensitivity and selectivity (Table 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of electrochemical detection of Hg2+ ions via NCD/GCE sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The cyclic voltammogram study revealed that the suggested sensor could detect Hg2+ ions preferentially. The effective detection of mercuric ions is due to a unique electron transfer interaction on the sensor's surface [34,35]. The oxidation and reduction processes are distinguished by two separate peak currents, both positive and negative. The negative peak current is caused by the reduction of Hg2+ ions, whereas the positive peak current is caused by the oxidation of Hg0. The potential electron transfer pathway for sensing the mercuric ion at the surface of the NCD/GCE sensor is depicted in Scheme 3 .

Real sample analysis was done to ensure the practicability of NCDs as a fluorescent probe for tetracycline detection and an electrochemical probe for Hg2+ detection. The initial and final fluorescence intensity values of NCD solution with and without tetracycline were recorded in the case of fluorescence-based detection. In the case of electrochemical detection of Hg2+, DPV analysis was carried out, and the current value was recorded. Tables 3 and 4
describe the results of each experiment, which were carried out three times. The RSD is less than 5%, and the recovery rates show that this sensor and analytical techniques may be used to sense analytes in real samples with reasonable accuracy. This sensor is non-toxic, environment friendly, and biocompatible, and it provides a variety of benefits. As a result, it can be used for real samples without creating environmental damage.

In conclusion, using the hydrothermal approach with Curcuma amada and triethylene tetraamine, highly fluorescent NCDs were developed to work as a fluorescent probe for tetracycline and an electrochemical probe for Hg2+ ion. The synthesized NCDs with green fluorescence were studied structurally and optically using various techniques. The fluorescent detection of tetracycline with an LOD of 32 nM was accomplished via an inner filter effect-based-fluorescence quenching mechanism. NCDs' superior electrochemical properties facilitated electrochemical sensing of Hg2+ ions with an LOD of 0.2 nM. The practicability of NCDs was tested using tap and river water samples. Finally, we proposed that these new NCDs, which were produced in a relatively easy manner, could be employed as a “turn-off” tetracycline fluorometric sensor and an electrochemical probe for Hg2+ ion detection.
Binila K Korah - Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology,Resources, Writing - original draft.
Mamatha Susan Punnoose - Data curation, Visualization.
Chinnu R Thara - Data curation, Visualization.
Dr. Thomas Abraham - Data curation, Visualization.
Dr. K. G. Ambady - Data curation, Visualization.
Dr. Beena Mathew - Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2022.108980.
